questions.question: Why is a short debrief after each lesson or mock valuable?
options: It converts a finished session into strengths, gaps, and scheduled next actions before the details fade.
It replaces the need to practice again on similar prompts.
It guarantees that every mistake was technical rather than communicative.
It removes the need for progress tracking.
questions.explanation: Debriefs preserve learning signal while it is still fresh.

questions.question: What is the most important property of a sustainable iteration cadence?
options: It revisits weak areas deliberately without creating an ever-growing impossible backlog.
It schedules every unresolved topic for the next day.
It prevents learners from repeating the same prompt.
It focuses only on favorite topics to maintain confidence.
questions.explanation: Good cadence balances reinforcement with realistic workload.

questions.question: Why should each debrief include a due date or retest plan?
options: Because unscheduled improvements are easy to agree with and easy to forget.
Because all lessons must be revisited within exactly 24 hours.
Because debriefs are primarily a calendar management tool.
Because interviewers score retest dates directly.
questions.explanation: Scheduling converts intention into an actual loop.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with capping the number of active follow-up drills?
options: It improves completion and focus, but some low-priority gaps will wait longer.
It makes weekly review impossible.
It removes the need for a mistake log.
It ensures all weaknesses disappear at the same rate.
questions.explanation: A small active queue is usually more effective than an exhaustive one.

questions.question: Which failure mode most often breaks an iteration cadence?
options: Debriefs capture too many possible next actions and none become the real priority.
The learner records one clear strength.
The session includes a retest date.
The progress tracker links back to the debrief.

questions.question: Which observability signal best reflects whether the cadence is working?
options: The delay between identifying a weak area and actually retesting it.
The number of total markdown files in the repo.
The average length of a lesson title.
The total number of architecture diagrams stored locally.
questions.explanation: Cadence quality shows up in how reliably weak areas are revisited.

questions.question: If a debrief says the architecture was fine but communication failed, what should happen next?
options: Schedule a drill that constrains explanation time and review structure instead of only rereading technical content.
Ignore the feedback because the technical design was correct.
Switch only to easier prompts.
Stop taking notes during mocks.
questions.explanation: The next drill should target the failure mode you actually observed.

questions.question: When interviews are very close, how should the cadence usually shift?
options: Toward tighter retest loops on the highest-leverage weak areas rather than broad new exploration.
Toward adding as many new lessons as possible.
Toward deleting all old debriefs for clarity.
Toward skipping feedback to conserve time.
questions.explanation: Near interviews, focused reinforcement usually beats breadth.
